author: Augustine of Hippo
book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.

classification.cautionReason: This work discusses heresy in order to refute it; do not classify it as heretical merely from its title.

lineage: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
That Righteousness is Never Perfected in This Life.
title: That Righteousness is Never Perfected in This Life.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: Now, according to this righteousness of God, that is, which we have from God, faith now worketh by love.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: But it worketh that, in what way man can attain to Him on whom now, not seeing, he believes; and when he shall see Him, then that which was in faith through a glass enigmatically, shall at length be in sight face to face; and then shall be perfected even love itself.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: Because it is said with excessive folly, that God is loved as much before He is seen, as He will be loved when He is seen.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: Further, if in this life, as no religious person doubts, the more we love God, so much the more righteous we certainly are, who can doubt that pious and true righteousness will then be perfected when the love of God shall be perfect?

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: Then the law, therefore, shall be fulfilled; so that nothing at all is wanting to it, of which law, according to the apostle, the fulfilling is Love.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: And thus, when he had said, “Not having my own righteousness, which is of the law, but that which is by the faith of Jesus Christ, which is the righteousness from God in faith,” he then added, “That I may know Him, and the power of His resurrection, and the fellowship of His sufferings.”

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: All these things were not yet full and perfect in the apostle; but, as if he were placed on the way, he was running towards their fulness and perfection.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: For how had he already perfectly known Christ, who says in another place, “Now I know in part; but then I shall know even as I am known”?

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: And how had he already perfectly known the power of His resurrection, to whom it remained to know it yet more fully by experience at the time of the resurrection of the flesh?

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: And how had he perfectly known already the fellowship of His suffering, if he had not yet experienced for him the suffering of death?

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: Finally, he adds and says, “If in any manner I may attain unto the resurrection of the dead.”

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: And then he says, “Not that I have already received or am already perfected.”

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: What, then, does he confess that he has not yet received, and in what is he not yet perfected, except that righteousness which is of God, which he desired, not willing to have his own righteousness, which is of the law?

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: For hence he was speaking, and such was the reason for his saying these things in resistance to the enemies of the grace of God, for the bestowal of which Christ was crucified; and of the race of whom are also these.
